title: The Sonnets LXX - That thou art blamï؟½d shall not be thy defect
content:
آ آ آ آ That thou art blamï؟½d shall not be thy defect,
آ آ آ آ For slanderï؟½s mark was ever yet the fair;
آ آ آ آ The ornament of beauty is suspect,
آ آ آ آ A crow that flies in heavenï؟½s sweetest air.
آ آ آ آ So thou be good, slander doth but approve
آ آ آ آ Thy worth the greater being wooï؟½d of time;
آ آ آ آ For canker vice the sweetest buds doth love,
آ آ آ آ And thou presentï؟½st a pure unstained prime.
آ آ آ آ Thou hast passed by the ambush of young days
آ آ آ آ Either not assailï؟½d, or victor being chargï؟½d;
آ آ آ آ Yet this thy praise cannot be so thy praise,
آ آ آ آ To tie up envy, evermore enlargï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ If some suspect of ill maskï؟½d not thy show,
آ آ آ آ Then thou alone kingdoms of hearts shouldst owe.
meaning:
"The Sonnets LXX - That thou art blamï؟½d shall not be thy defect" is a quintessential example of William Shakespeare's signature style...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
